headline: Lahad Datu Anniversary: 13 Years Without Permanent Security Legislation
context: The 2013 Lahad Datu incursion killed at least 68 people. ESSCOM was created in response but still operates under executive instruments — no Act of Parliament in 13 years. Its forward base is 41% complete at 54% over budget.

cards.big: ESSCOM marks 13 years: zero kidnappings since 2020, but its forward base is 41% complete and it still has no Act of Parliament
cards.sub: The 2013 Lahad Datu incursion killed at least 68 people. ESSCOM was created by executive directive on 1 April 2013 and has never been codified into law

cards.big: 59 kidnapping attempts foiled and zero cases since January 2020 — though decline also reflects Philippine ops against Abu Sayyaf and COVID border closures
cards.sub: Pulau Mabul forward base only 41% complete after a decade, costs rose from RM52.81M to RM81.51M after contractor failure

cards.big: Three successive administrations have kept ESSCOM on executive instruments without passing a dedicated Act of Parliament
cards.sub: The maritime curfew began July 2014 and has been in effect for nearly 12 years, affecting B40 fishermen and women traders in coastal economies

cards.big: The security zone covers 1,400 km of coastline alongside 1.04 million non-citizens in a state where poverty runs at 19.5% versus the national 6.2%
cards.sub: Communities of Sulu, Bajau, and Filipino origin face heightened scrutiny while Sabah leaders argue federal commitments remain unfulfilled

cards.big: The question is not whether ESSCOM works — it does. The question is why no government has given it the permanent legal mandate its mission demands
Think of it like a hospital saving lives from a tent for 13 years while the permanent building sits 41% complete
cards.sub: If this were a security zone in the Klang Valley, the language would be national scandal — in Sabah, it is framed as a regional grievance

cards.big: ESSCOM is a genuine security success on structurally fragile foundations. Three administrations have maintained its temporary status — that is a system failure, not one government's neglect
